name: Judgment deities
shortDescription: Deities in Zoroastrianism responsible for judging the souls of the dead and determining their fate in the afterlife.
description: In Zoroastrianism, the judgment of the soul after death is a crucial event, overseen by several deities who ensure justice and determine the soul's destiny. Ahura Mazda, the supreme god, is ultimately the source of justice, but he delegates specific roles to other divine beings. Mithra, Sraosha, and Rashnu are central figures in this process. Mithra acts as a mediator and judge, weighing the good and bad deeds of the deceased. Sraosha, representing obedience and righteousness, guides the soul and assists in the judgment. Rashnu is the most rigorously just, known for his unwavering impartiality and precision in assessing the soul's merits. The Chinvat Bridge serves as the location for this judgment, a symbolic bridge that narrows for the wicked and widens for the righteous, leading to either paradise or hell. The concept of Asha, representing truth and cosmic order, is fundamental to the judgment, as the deities strive to uphold this principle in their decisions. The judgment ensures that individuals are held accountable for their actions during their earthly lives, emphasizing the importance of moral behavior and adherence to Zoroastrian principles. The process reflects the dualistic nature of Zoroastrianism, highlighting the constant struggle between good and evil and the ultimate triumph of righteousness.

sources.title: The Zend-Avesta, Part I (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 4)
sources.author: James Darmesteter (Translator)

sources.title: The Religion of the Good: Zoroastrianism
sources.author: Patricia Crone
